seo.title: SQL String Functions Tutorial - CONCAT, SUBSTRING, UPPER, TRIM, REPLACE | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master MySQL string functions with real examples. Learn CONCAT, CONCAT_WS, SUBSTRING, LEFT, RIGHT, UPPER, LOWER, TRIM, REPLACE, LOCATE, LENGTH vs CHAR_LENGTH, LPAD, RPAD. Includes 60+ practice questions on cleaning and formatting text data.
seo.keywords: sql string functions, mysql string functions, concat sql, substring sql, upper lower sql, sql trim, sql replace, sql locate, char_length vs length, mysql string manipulation, sql text functions

content.what:
What Are SQL String Functions?

String functions transform and extract information from text (VARCHAR, TEXT, CHAR) values. Real-world data is messy: names with extra spaces, inconsistent capitalization, phone numbers with random separators, emails in mixed case. Before you can report on it, you have to clean it — and clean SQL string functions are the way.

MySQL ships dozens of string functions. This chapter covers the 20 or so that you will actually use every week as a data analyst or backend developer.

Sample Table Used Throughout This Chapter

CREATE TABLE customers (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    full_name VARCHAR(100),
    email VARCHAR(100),
    phone VARCHAR(20),
    city VARCHAR(50)
);

INSERT INTO customers VALUES
(1, '  Aarav Kumar  ',    'Aarav.Kumar@Gmail.COM', '+91-98765-43210',  'Bengaluru'),
(2, 'priya SHARMA',       'priya@MAIL.com',        '+91 9876 543 211', 'mumbai'),
(3, 'Rohan  Mehta',       'ROHAN@mail.com',        '9876543212',       'Delhi'),
(4, 'meera-iyer',         'meera@MAIL.com',        '091-9876-543213',  'chennai'),
(5, 'Vikram Singh Rajput','v.singh@MAIL.CO.IN',    '+91.9876.543.214', 'Pune');

This is the kind of real data you will see in actual databases: leading/trailing spaces, inconsistent casing, irregular phone formatting, mixed-case emails. Let's clean it.
content.why:
Why String Functions Matter

1. Every Real Database Has Dirty Text

User-entered data is always messy. Even 'clean' data from APIs has quirks. Before you can match, group, or report, you need normalization: trim spaces, lowercase emails, format phone numbers consistently. String functions let you do this at the database level, often faster than in application code.

2. Searching and Matching Depends on It

Equality comparisons on VARCHAR are case-sensitive in many collations. Email validation, name matching, deduplication — all need LOWER() or TRIM() or both. If you do not normalize, 'Aarav Kumar' and 'aarav kumar' are different customers to SQL, which is wrong.

3. Reporting Requires Formatting

Concatenate first and last names for display. Mask credit card numbers showing only the last 4 digits. Format phone numbers for printing. Truncate long descriptions. All of this is string function work.

4. Interview Questions Use String Functions

'Extract the domain from an email,' 'Get the first name,' 'Format names in proper case,' 'Find all customers with a specific prefix,' 'Count occurrences of a character.' These appear in real SQL interviews regularly.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. CONCAT and CONCAT_WS - Joining Strings

SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' ', 'World');           -- 'Hello World'
SELECT CONCAT('User: ', id, ', Name: ', full_name) FROM customers;

-- CONCAT_WS: CONCAT With Separator
SELECT CONCAT_WS(', ', 'Aarav', 'Bengaluru', 'India');  -- 'Aarav, Bengaluru, India'
SELECT CONCAT_WS(' - ', first_name, middle_name, last_name) FROM people;

Gotcha: CONCAT returns NULL if ANY argument is NULL. CONCAT_WS skips NULL arguments gracefully (but returns NULL only if the separator itself is NULL).

2. LENGTH vs CHAR_LENGTH

This matters for non-ASCII text:

  • LENGTH(str): returns the number of bytes.
  • CHAR_LENGTH(str) (or CHARACTER_LENGTH): returns the number of characters.
SELECT LENGTH('hello');            -- 5 bytes
SELECT CHAR_LENGTH('hello');       -- 5 chars

SELECT LENGTH('नमस्ते');           -- 18 bytes (UTF-8)
SELECT CHAR_LENGTH('नमस्ते');       -- 6 chars

For anything user-facing (validating max length, counting), use CHAR_LENGTH. LENGTH is for storage calculations.

3. UPPER, LOWER, INITCAP

SELECT UPPER('hello');             -- 'HELLO'
SELECT LOWER('HELLO');             -- 'hello'
-- INITCAP capitalizes the first letter of each word
-- Available in PostgreSQL and Oracle; NOT in MySQL
-- MySQL workaround using functions and string manipulation shown later

Use LOWER() for case-insensitive comparisons (on case-sensitive collations):

-- Find all variants of an email
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE LOWER(email) = 'priya@mail.com';

4. SUBSTRING (SUBSTR) - Extract a Portion

Syntax: SUBSTRING(str, start, length) or SUBSTRING(str FROM start FOR length). Position is 1-based in MySQL.

SELECT SUBSTRING('Bengaluru', 1, 4);       -- 'Beng'
SELECT SUBSTRING('Bengaluru', 4);          -- 'galuru' (from position 4 to end)
SELECT SUBSTR('Bengaluru', -4);            -- 'luru' (last 4 chars, negative = from end)

5. LEFT and RIGHT

SELECT LEFT('Bengaluru', 4);    -- 'Beng'
SELECT RIGHT('Bengaluru', 4);   -- 'luru'

-- Get last 4 digits of a phone number
SELECT RIGHT(phone, 4) FROM customers;

LEFT/RIGHT are more readable than SUBSTRING when you want a prefix or suffix.

6. TRIM, LTRIM, RTRIM

-- Remove whitespace from both ends
SELECT TRIM('  Aarav Kumar  ');         -- 'Aarav Kumar'
-- Left only
SELECT LTRIM('  Aarav');                -- 'Aarav'
-- Right only
SELECT RTRIM('Aarav  ');                -- 'Aarav'
-- Remove specific characters
SELECT TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM '00012345');  -- '12345'
SELECT TRIM(TRAILING '.' FROM 'hello...');  -- 'hello'
SELECT TRIM(BOTH '-' FROM '---abc---');     -- 'abc'

For cleaning messy input: TRIM() before comparison and before storage.

7. REPLACE - Substitute Substrings

SELECT REPLACE('+91-98765-43210', '-', '');   -- '+919876543210'
SELECT REPLACE(email, '@gmail.com', '@GMAIL.COM') FROM customers;
SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'SQL'); -- 'Hello SQL'

REPLACE is case-sensitive. To replace case-insensitively in MySQL, you need REGEXP_REPLACE (MySQL 8.0+).

8. LOCATE, INSTR, POSITION - Find a Substring

All three do the same thing with slightly different argument orders. Return the 1-based position of a substring, or 0 if not found.

SELECT LOCATE('@', 'aarav@mail.com');   -- 6
SELECT INSTR('aarav@mail.com', '@');   -- 6
SELECT POSITION('@' IN 'aarav@mail.com'); -- 6

-- LOCATE with start position
SELECT LOCATE('a', 'banana', 3);   -- finds 'a' starting from position 3

Combined with SUBSTRING to extract parts:

-- Extract the domain from an email
SELECT SUBSTRING(email, LOCATE('@', email) + 1)
FROM customers;

9. LPAD, RPAD - Padding

SELECT LPAD('42', 5, '0');     -- '00042'
SELECT RPAD('abc', 6, '.');    -- 'abc...'

-- Format IDs as 6-digit codes
SELECT LPAD(id, 6, '0') FROM products;
-- 1 -> '000001', 42 -> '000042'

10. REVERSE, REPEAT

SELECT REVERSE('hello');     -- 'olleh'
SELECT REPEAT('ab', 4);      -- 'abababab'

REVERSE is occasionally useful (palindrome checks, finding the last delimiter). REPEAT is common in generating test data or visual separators.

11. FORMAT - Numeric to Thousands-Separated String

SELECT FORMAT(1234567.891, 2);     -- '1,234,567.89'
SELECT FORMAT(1234567.891, 0);     -- '1,234,568'

Handy for report output. Note: the result is a string.

12. Cleaning the Sample Customer Data

-- Clean the messy customer data into a presentable form
SELECT
    id,
    TRIM(REPLACE(REPLACE(full_name, '  ', ' '), '-', ' ')) AS clean_name,
    LOWER(email) AS clean_email,
    REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(phone, '-', ''), ' ', ''), '.', ''), '+', '') AS clean_phone,
    CONCAT(UPPER(LEFT(city, 1)), LOWER(SUBSTRING(city, 2))) AS proper_city
FROM customers;

Expected output:

+----+--------------------+-----------------------+------------------+-----------+
| id | clean_name         | clean_email           | clean_phone      | proper_city|
+----+--------------------+-----------------------+------------------+-----------+
|  1 | Aarav Kumar        | aarav.kumar@gmail.com | 919876543210     | Bengaluru |
|  2 | priya SHARMA       | priya@mail.com        | 919876543211     | Mumbai    |
|  3 | Rohan Mehta        | rohan@mail.com        | 9876543212       | Delhi     |
|  4 | meera iyer         | meera@mail.com        | 0919876543213    | Chennai   |
|  5 | Vikram Singh Rajput| v.singh@mail.co.in    | 919876543214     | Pune      |
+----+--------------------+-----------------------+------------------+-----------+

Note: we did not fix capitalization of names here. MySQL lacks INITCAP, so proper-case names require substring manipulation or user-defined functions.

13. Extract First Name - A Real Pattern

-- Everything before the first space
SELECT
    full_name,
    TRIM(SUBSTRING(TRIM(full_name), 1, LOCATE(' ', TRIM(full_name)) - 1)) AS first_name
FROM customers;

-- Handle the case where there is no space (single-word name)
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN LOCATE(' ', TRIM(full_name)) > 0
        THEN SUBSTRING(TRIM(full_name), 1, LOCATE(' ', TRIM(full_name)) - 1)
        ELSE TRIM(full_name)
    END AS first_name
FROM customers;

14. Mask Sensitive Data

-- Show only last 4 digits of phone
SELECT CONCAT(REPEAT('X', CHAR_LENGTH(phone) - 4), RIGHT(phone, 4)) AS masked
FROM customers;

-- Show first 2 letters of email + xxxxx + domain
SELECT CONCAT(
    LEFT(email, 2),
    REPEAT('x', 5),
    SUBSTRING(email, LOCATE('@', email))
) AS masked_email
FROM customers;

codeExamples.title: CONCAT vs CONCAT_WS - Joining Values
codeExamples.code:
SELECT
    id,
    CONCAT(full_name, ' <', email, '>') AS contact_card,
    CONCAT_WS(' | ', full_name, email, phone, city) AS summary
FROM customers
LIMIT 3;
codeExamples.explanation: CONCAT joins arguments with no separator. CONCAT_WS (With Separator) takes a separator as the first argument and inserts it between the rest. CONCAT_WS also skips NULL values; CONCAT returns NULL if any argument is NULL.

codeExamples.title: Clean Messy Names with TRIM and REPLACE
codeExamples.code:
SELECT
    id,
    full_name AS original,
    -- Collapse multiple spaces, trim, replace dashes
    TRIM(REPLACE(REPLACE(full_name, '  ', ' '), '-', ' ')) AS cleaned,
    CHAR_LENGTH(TRIM(full_name)) AS clean_length
FROM customers;
codeExamples.explanation: REPLACE(..., ' ', ' ') collapses double spaces (note: does not handle 3+ spaces in one pass). REPLACE(..., '-', ' ') turns dashes into spaces. TRIM removes leading/trailing whitespace. CHAR_LENGTH shows the character count after cleaning.

codeExamples.title: Extract Email Domain Using LOCATE and SUBSTRING
codeExamples.code:
SELECT
    email,
    SUBSTRING(email, 1, LOCATE('@', email) - 1) AS username,
    SUBSTRING(email, LOCATE('@', email) + 1)   AS domain
FROM customers;
codeExamples.explanation: LOCATE('@', email) returns the position of '@' (1-based). SUBSTRING with start and length extracts the username before '@'. Starting from LOCATE('@', email) + 1 without a length gives everything after '@' — the domain. LOWER(domain) would normalize it.
codeExamples.output:
+----------------------+--------------+------------+
| email                | username     | domain     |
+----------------------+--------------+------------+
| Aarav.Kumar@Gmail.COM| Aarav.Kumar  | Gmail.COM  |
| priya@MAIL.com       | priya        | MAIL.com   |
| ROHAN@mail.com       | ROHAN        | mail.com   |
| meera@MAIL.com       | meera        | MAIL.com   |
| v.singh@MAIL.CO.IN   | v.singh      | MAIL.CO.IN |
+----------------------+--------------+------------+
5 rows in set

codeExamples.title: Extract First Name Before First Space
codeExamples.code:
SELECT
    full_name,
    TRIM(SUBSTRING(
        TRIM(full_name),
        1,
        CASE
            WHEN LOCATE(' ', TRIM(full_name)) = 0 THEN CHAR_LENGTH(TRIM(full_name))
            ELSE LOCATE(' ', TRIM(full_name)) - 1
        END
    )) AS first_name
FROM customers;
codeExamples.explanation: Trim first to remove leading spaces. Use LOCATE to find the first space. If there is no space (single-word name), take the whole string. The CASE handles both cases safely. SUBSTRING extracts chars 1 through space-position-1.
codeExamples.output:
+----------------------+------------+
| full_name            | first_name |
+----------------------+------------+
|   Aarav Kumar        | Aarav      |
| priya SHARMA         | priya      |
| Rohan  Mehta         | Rohan      |
| meera-iyer           | meera-iyer |
| Vikram Singh Rajput  | Vikram     |
+----------------------+------------+
5 rows in set
-- 'meera-iyer' has no space, so entire string returned

codeExamples.title: Format Phone Numbers - Remove All Separators
codeExamples.code:
SELECT
    phone AS original,
    REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(phone, '-', ''), ' ', ''), '.', ''), '(', ''), ')', '') AS digits_only,
    RIGHT(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(phone, '-', ''), ' ', ''), '.', ''), '+', ''), '0', ''), 10) AS normalized_10_digit
FROM customers;
codeExamples.explanation: Chain REPLACE calls to strip every non-digit separator. The normalized version removes '+' and leading zeros, then takes the last 10 digits. For production, MySQL 8.0+ has REGEXP_REPLACE which is cleaner: REGEXP_REPLACE(phone, '[^0-9]', '').

codeExamples.title: LENGTH vs CHAR_LENGTH - Unicode Matters
codeExamples.code:
SELECT
    'Hello' AS str,
    LENGTH('Hello')      AS byte_length,
    CHAR_LENGTH('Hello') AS char_length
UNION ALL
SELECT
    'नमस्ते',
    LENGTH('नमस्ते'),
    CHAR_LENGTH('नमस्ते')
UNION ALL
SELECT
    '  Aarav  ',
    LENGTH('  Aarav  '),
    CHAR_LENGTH('  Aarav  ');
codeExamples.explanation: For ASCII text, LENGTH and CHAR_LENGTH are equal. For UTF-8 text containing multi-byte characters (Hindi, Chinese, emoji), LENGTH counts bytes (often 3-4 per character) while CHAR_LENGTH counts characters. Always use CHAR_LENGTH for user-facing length limits.

codeExamples.title: LPAD for ID Formatting
codeExamples.explanation: LPAD(value, target_length, pad_char) pads on the left. Useful for fixed-width product codes, invoice numbers, order IDs. CONCAT adds a prefix like 'PRD-' for a branded SKU format.

codeExamples.title: Case-Insensitive Email Deduplication
codeExamples.code:
-- Find emails where the lowercase form appears more than once
SELECT
    LOWER(email) AS normalized_email,
    COUNT(*)     AS occurrences,
    GROUP_CONCAT(email) AS original_forms
FROM customers
GROUP BY LOWER(email)
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
codeExamples.explanation: Real-world duplicates often differ only in case: Aarav@GMAIL.com vs aarav@gmail.com. Normalize with LOWER(), then GROUP BY and HAVING COUNT > 1 to find duplicates. GROUP_CONCAT shows the original variants.
codeExamples.output:
+------------------+-------------+----------------+
| normalized_email | occurrences | original_forms |
+------------------+-------------+----------------+
-- On sample data: no duplicates after normalization.
-- If you had aarav@gmail.com inserted twice with different cases, it would show up here.
+------------------+-------------+----------------+
0 rows in set

commonMistakes.title: Using LENGTH When You Mean CHAR_LENGTH
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
-- Validate that a username is 5-15 characters
SELECT * FROM users WHERE LENGTH(username) BETWEEN 5 AND 15;
commonMistakes.error: No error, but the check is wrong for non-ASCII names. A 5-character Hindi username might be 15 bytes (stored as UTF-8), so it incorrectly appears within the range when it should be caught as too short — or vice versa.
commonMistakes.correctCode: SELECT * FROM users WHERE CHAR_LENGTH(username) BETWEEN 5 AND 15;
commonMistakes.explanation: CHAR_LENGTH counts characters (what users see). LENGTH counts bytes (what storage uses). For user-facing validation, always use CHAR_LENGTH.

commonMistakes.title: CONCAT With NULL Returns NULL
commonMistakes.error: No error, but the result is NULL for every person with a NULL middle name.
commonMistakes.explanation: CONCAT returns NULL if ANY argument is NULL. CONCAT_WS safely skips NULL arguments (unless the separator itself is NULL). COALESCE is another way to provide a default.

commonMistakes.title: SUBSTRING With Off-by-One Error
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
-- Intent: get the first 5 characters
SELECT SUBSTRING('Bengaluru', 0, 5);  -- returns '' in MySQL!
commonMistakes.error: No error but returns an empty string or unexpected content. MySQL treats position 0 as special.
commonMistakes.explanation: MySQL's SUBSTRING is 1-indexed. Position 1 is the first character. Position 0 is undefined or treated as 'before the start'. Use LEFT(str, n) when you want a prefix — it is more readable.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting REPLACE Is Case-Sensitive
commonMistakes.error: No error, but case mismatches are not replaced.
commonMistakes.explanation: REPLACE does exact (case-sensitive) substring matching. For case-insensitive replacement, either normalize the case first or use REGEXP_REPLACE with the 'i' flag in MySQL 8.0+.

summary: CONCAT joins strings with no separator. CONCAT_WS takes a separator and safely skips NULL arguments. CONCAT returns NULL if any argument is NULL.
LENGTH returns bytes; CHAR_LENGTH returns characters. For user-facing validation and Unicode text, use CHAR_LENGTH.
UPPER and LOWER change case. MySQL has no INITCAP — proper case requires manual substring manipulation or a UDF.
SUBSTRING(str, start, length) is 1-indexed. LEFT(str, n) and RIGHT(str, n) are more readable for prefixes and suffixes.
TRIM removes whitespace; LTRIM and RTRIM remove from one side. TRIM with LEADING/TRAILING/BOTH and a specific character cleans other padding.
REPLACE is case-sensitive. For case-insensitive replacement in MySQL 8.0+, use REGEXP_REPLACE with the 'i' flag.
LOCATE, INSTR, POSITION all find a substring and return 1-based position (0 if not found). Combine with SUBSTRING to extract parts like email domains.
LPAD and RPAD add padding to make strings a fixed length. Common use: formatting IDs like '000042' or SKUs like 'PRD-000042'.
REVERSE and REPEAT are niche. FORMAT(n, d) formats a number with thousands separators and d decimals — returns a string.
For cleaning data: TRIM then REPLACE to remove bad characters, LOWER for normalized comparison, CONCAT_WS for NULL-safe joining.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' ', 'World');
topicSpecific.explanation: CONCAT concatenates all arguments in order with no separator.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT CONCAT_WS('-', '2026', '04', '16');
topicSpecific.explanation: CONCAT_WS inserts the separator (first arg) between each of the following arguments.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', NULL, 'World');
topicSpecific.explanation: CONCAT returns NULL if any argument is NULL. Use CONCAT_WS which skips NULLs, or COALESCE to provide defaults.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT CONCAT_WS('-', '2026', NULL, '16');
topicSpecific.explanation: CONCAT_WS skips NULL arguments. Result is '2026' + '-' + '16'.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT UPPER('Bengaluru'), LOWER('MUMBAI');
topicSpecific.explanation: UPPER uppercases all letters. LOWER lowercases them.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT LENGTH('Hello'), CHAR_LENGTH('Hello');
topicSpecific.explanation: ASCII characters are single-byte in UTF-8, so LENGTH and CHAR_LENGTH match.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT LEFT('Bengaluru', 4), RIGHT('Bengaluru', 4);
topicSpecific.explanation: LEFT(str, n) returns the first n characters. RIGHT(str, n) returns the last n characters.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT TRIM('  Aarav Kumar  '), CHAR_LENGTH(TRIM('  Aarav Kumar  '));
topicSpecific.hint: TRIM removes whitespace from both ends.
topicSpecific.explanation: TRIM removes leading/trailing spaces. The trimmed string has 11 characters including the internal space.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT REPLACE('+91-98765-43210', '-', '');
topicSpecific.explanation: REPLACE substitutes every occurrence of the second argument with the third. Here, all dashes are removed.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT LOCATE('@', 'aarav@mail.com');
topicSpecific.explanation: '@' is the 6th character. Positions: a=1, a=2, r=3, a=4, v=5, @=6.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT SUBSTRING('Bengaluru', 4, 3);
topicSpecific.explanation: From position 4, take 3 characters: g, a, l. Positions: B=1, e=2, n=3, g=4, a=5, l=6.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT LPAD('42', 5, '0'), RPAD('abc', 6, '.');
topicSpecific.explanation: LPAD pads on the left to length 5: three zeros + '42'. RPAD pads on the right: 'abc' + three dots.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT SUBSTRING('aarav@mail.com', LOCATE('@', 'aarav@mail.com') + 1);
topicSpecific.explanation: LOCATE finds '@' at position 6. +1 gives 7. SUBSTRING from position 7 with no length returns everything from there to the end.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM '00012345');
topicSpecific.hint: LEADING removes from the left only.
topicSpecific.explanation: TRIM with LEADING removes the specified character only from the beginning. All leading zeros are stripped; internal zeros (if any) are preserved.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT REVERSE('hello');
topicSpecific.explanation: REVERSE reverses the character order. Works on multi-byte characters correctly in recent MySQL versions.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT REPEAT('ab', 3);
topicSpecific.explanation: REPEAT(str, n) concatenates the string n times. Useful for generating separators or padding.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT FORMAT(1234567.89, 2);
topicSpecific.explanation: FORMAT adds thousands separators (commas by default) and formats to the given number of decimal places. Returns a string.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT SUBSTR('Bengaluru', -4);
topicSpecific.explanation: Negative start counts from the end. -4 means start at the 4th character from the end. Equivalent to RIGHT('Bengaluru', 4).

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
SELECT LENGTH('नमस्ते'), CHAR_LENGTH('नमस्ते');
topicSpecific.explanation: 'नमस्ते' has 6 visible characters. Each Devanagari character is 3 bytes in UTF-8, so 6 * 3 = 18 bytes. LENGTH shows bytes, CHAR_LENGTH shows characters.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output of this clean query on ' Aarav Kumar '?
SELECT TRIM(REPLACE('  Aarav  Kumar  ', '  ', ' '));
topicSpecific.explanation: REPLACE turns '  ' into ' ' in one pass: '  Aarav  Kumar  ' becomes ' Aarav Kumar '. TRIM strips the remaining leading/trailing single space. For strings with 3+ consecutive spaces, multiple REPLACE passes may be needed.

mixed.question: Which function joins strings safely when any argument might be NULL?
mixed.answer: CONCAT_WS (skips NULL arguments). CONCAT returns NULL if any argument is NULL.
mixed.explanation: CONCAT_WS stands for 'With Separator.' Takes separator first, then values. NULLs in the values are skipped.

mixed.question: Is MySQL's REPLACE case-sensitive?
mixed.answer: Yes. REPLACE does case-sensitive substring replacement. For case-insensitive, use REGEXP_REPLACE with the 'i' flag in MySQL 8.0+, or normalize case first.
mixed.explanation: This is a common bug source. 'Gmail' and 'gmail' are different to REPLACE.

mixed.question: What does LENGTH('हi') return in MySQL with UTF-8 encoding?
mixed.answer: 4 (3 bytes for ह + 1 byte for i)
mixed.explanation: LENGTH counts bytes. 'ह' is 3 bytes in UTF-8. 'i' is 1 byte. Total 4. CHAR_LENGTH would return 2.

mixed.question: What is the difference between SUBSTRING(str, 1, 5) and LEFT(str, 5)?
mixed.answer: They are equivalent. LEFT is more readable for prefixes.
mixed.explanation: Both return the first 5 characters. LEFT is preferred when you specifically want a prefix because it's clearer at a glance.

mixed.question: Write a query to return the last 4 characters of a phone number stored in phone column.
mixed.explanation: RIGHT(str, n) returns the last n characters. Commonly used for masking credit cards, showing truncated tokens, etc.

mixed.question: Write a query to extract the email domain (part after @) from every customer.
mixed.answer:
SELECT email, SUBSTRING(email, LOCATE('@', email) + 1) AS domain
FROM customers;
mixed.explanation: LOCATE finds '@'. Add 1 to skip past it. SUBSTRING without a length argument returns everything from there to the end.

mixed.question: Normalize city names so they have proper case: first letter uppercase, rest lowercase.
mixed.answer:
SELECT city, CONCAT(UPPER(LEFT(city, 1)), LOWER(SUBSTRING(city, 2))) AS proper_city
FROM customers;
mixed.explanation: MySQL lacks INITCAP. Workaround: uppercase first letter with LEFT+UPPER, lowercase the rest with SUBSTRING+LOWER, concatenate. Only handles single-word strings; multi-word requires further work.

mixed.question: Write a query to remove all non-digit characters from a phone column using REPLACE.
mixed.explanation: REPLACE cleans one character at a time. Chain multiple REPLACE calls for each separator. In MySQL 8.0+ use REGEXP_REPLACE(phone, '[^0-9]', '') for a single-call solution.

mixed.question: When should you use CHAR_LENGTH over LENGTH?
mixed.answer: Always use CHAR_LENGTH for user-facing length operations (validation, truncation). Use LENGTH only when you care about byte storage (e.g., checking maximum byte size for a column).
mixed.explanation: LENGTH counts bytes. For Unicode languages (Hindi, Chinese, Japanese, emoji), one character can be 2-4 bytes. CHAR_LENGTH always reports what users see.

mixed.question: Why is CONCAT_WS often preferred over CONCAT?
mixed.answer: CONCAT_WS inserts a separator automatically and skips NULL arguments. CONCAT returns NULL if any argument is NULL and requires manually adding separators between arguments.
mixed.explanation: CONCAT_WS is especially useful for addresses and names where some parts may be NULL (middle_name, address_line_2).

mixed.question: Write a query that masks email so only the first 2 characters and the domain show, e.g., 'aa***@mail.com'.
mixed.answer:
SELECT
    email,
    CONCAT(
        LEFT(email, 2),
        REPEAT('*', 3),
        SUBSTRING(email, LOCATE('@', email))
    ) AS masked
FROM customers;
mixed.explanation: LEFT grabs first 2 chars. REPEAT makes 3 stars. SUBSTRING from LOCATE('@',...) keeps '@' and everything after. Adjust star count for different masking widths.

mixed.question: Write a query to count the number of spaces in each full_name.
mixed.answer:
SELECT
    full_name,
    CHAR_LENGTH(full_name) - CHAR_LENGTH(REPLACE(full_name, ' ', '')) AS space_count
FROM customers;
mixed.explanation: Classic trick: subtract the length after removing all spaces from the original length. The difference equals the number of removed spaces. Works for counting any character.

mcqs.question: What does CONCAT return if one argument is NULL?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. CONCAT returns NULL if any argument is NULL. Use CONCAT_WS or COALESCE to handle NULLs safely.

mcqs.question: Which returns the number of characters (not bytes)?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. CHAR_LENGTH (aka CHARACTER_LENGTH) counts characters. LENGTH counts bytes.

mcqs.question: SELECT SUBSTRING('Bengaluru', 4, 3); returns:
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Starting at position 4 ('g'), take 3 characters: 'gal'. MySQL SUBSTRING is 1-indexed.

mcqs.question: Which function removes whitespace from both ends?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. TRIM removes from both sides by default. LTRIM is left only, RTRIM is right only. STRIP is not a MySQL function.

mcqs.question: SELECT LPAD('5', 4, '0'); returns:
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. LPAD pads on the left to length 4 with '0', giving '0005'. Useful for ID formatting.

mcqs.question: Which returns the 1-based position of a substring (or 0 if not found)?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. LOCATE(substr, str) returns the 1-based position. INSTR and POSITION do the same thing with different argument orders.

mcqs.question: Is MySQL's REPLACE case-sensitive?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. REPLACE does exact case-sensitive matching. For case-insensitive, use REGEXP_REPLACE with the 'i' flag (MySQL 8.0+).

mcqs.question: SELECT CONCAT_WS('-', 'a', NULL, 'c'); returns:
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. CONCAT_WS skips NULL arguments. The separator is placed only between actual values, yielding 'a-c'.

mcqs.question: What is the output of SELECT LENGTH('नमस्ते'), CHAR_LENGTH('नमस्ते'); in UTF-8?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Each Devanagari character is 3 bytes in UTF-8. LENGTH = 6 * 3 = 18 bytes. CHAR_LENGTH = 6 characters.

mcqs.question: Which query gets the email domain (part after '@')?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Start one position after '@', no length = to end. A returns the username part + '@'. B returns '@domain'. D is wrong.

mcqs.question: SELECT TRIM(LEADING '0' FROM '000042'); returns:
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. TRIM LEADING removes the specified character only from the left. All leading zeros stripped.

mcqs.question: Count the occurrences of 'a' in 'banana' using only string functions.
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Subtract the length after removing the target character from the original length. Works for counting any character.

mcqs.question: Which is the safest way to join first, middle, and last names where middle can be NULL?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. CONCAT_WS skips NULL middle name. A returns NULL if middle is NULL. B and D are invalid syntax for string concatenation in MySQL.

mcqs.question: How do you format an integer id as a 6-digit zero-padded code?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. LPAD pads on the left to reach 6 characters with '0'. RPAD would pad on the right. FORMAT adds thousands separators and decimals, not zero padding.

mcqs.question: Which approach correctly finds duplicate emails ignoring case?

options.A: SELECT email FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
options.B: SELECT LOWER(email) FROM users GROUP BY LOWER(email) HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
options.C: SELECT email FROM users WHERE UPPER(email) = LOWER(email)

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Normalize by LOWER before grouping. A would miss duplicates differing in case. C is a nonsense condition. D just returns unique cases.

codingChallenges.title: Clean Up Name Column
codingChallenges.description: Given the customers table, return id and full_name trimmed of leading/trailing spaces.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT id, TRIM(full_name) AS clean_name
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: TRIM removes whitespace from both ends. Use it for cleaning user input before comparing or displaying.

codingChallenges.title: Normalize Email to Lowercase
codingChallenges.description: Return id and email converted to lowercase for all customers.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT id, LOWER(email) AS normalized_email
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: LOWER normalizes email case. This is essential before comparing or deduplicating user emails.

codingChallenges.title: Extract Email Username and Domain
codingChallenges.description: For each customer, return the email, the username (before '@'), and the domain (after '@').
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
    email,
    SUBSTRING(email, 1, LOCATE('@', email) - 1) AS username,
    SUBSTRING(email, LOCATE('@', email) + 1)   AS domain
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: LOCATE finds '@'. SUBSTRING extracts username (1 to @-1) and domain (@+1 to end).

testCases.output: email split into username and domain

codingChallenges.title: Digits-Only Phone Numbers
codingChallenges.description: Strip all separators (-, space, ., +) from phone numbers and return just the digits.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
    phone AS original,
    REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(phone, '-', ''), ' ', ''), '.', ''), '+', '') AS digits_only
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: Chain REPLACE to remove each separator. In MySQL 8.0+ use REGEXP_REPLACE(phone, '[^0-9]', '') for a single-call solution.

testCases.input: Sample phone numbers with mixed separators

codingChallenges.title: Proper Case City Names
codingChallenges.description: Capitalize the first letter of each city name, rest lowercase, e.g., 'bengaluru' -> 'Bengaluru'.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
    city AS original,
    CONCAT(UPPER(LEFT(city, 1)), LOWER(SUBSTRING(city, 2))) AS proper_city
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: MySQL lacks INITCAP. Take first char with LEFT and uppercase it; take rest with SUBSTRING from position 2 and lowercase it; concatenate.

codingChallenges.title: Count Spaces in Name
codingChallenges.description: Return the number of space characters in each full_name.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
    full_name,
    CHAR_LENGTH(full_name) - CHAR_LENGTH(REPLACE(full_name, ' ', '')) AS space_count
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: Classic trick: length minus length-without-spaces equals the number of spaces. Generalizes to counting any character.

codingChallenges.title: Mask Phone Number (Last 4 Digits Only)
codingChallenges.description: Mask all but the last 4 digits of a phone number with 'X'. Input format may vary.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
    phone,
    CONCAT(
        REPEAT('X', CHAR_LENGTH(phone) - 4),
        RIGHT(phone, 4)
    ) AS masked
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: REPEAT('X', n) creates n masking characters, where n is the length minus 4. RIGHT takes the last 4 characters. CONCAT joins them.

codingChallenges.title: Extract First Name (Before First Space)
codingChallenges.description: Given full_name, return just the first name (portion before first space). Handle names without spaces by returning the whole name.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
    full_name,
    TRIM(
        CASE
            WHEN LOCATE(' ', TRIM(full_name)) = 0
                THEN TRIM(full_name)
            ELSE SUBSTRING(TRIM(full_name), 1, LOCATE(' ', TRIM(full_name)) - 1)
        END
    ) AS first_name
FROM customers;
codingChallenges.explanation: Trim first to handle leading spaces. Use LOCATE to find first space. If no space (LOCATE returns 0), return the whole name. Otherwise take characters 1 through space-position-1.
